about.title: About me
about.personalText: I have been developing my skills since 2021, after completing my initial training. Currently I'm studying computer science at the <0>University of Bremen</0> and working part-time at <1>Rheinmetall</1> as a Software Developer. I specialize in web development using <2>TypeScript</2>, <2>React</2>, and <2>Angular</2>.
about.skillsSubtitle: Click on a skill to learn more

typescript.name: TypeScript
typescript.description: Back in 2021, during my apprenticeship, a friend introduced me to TypeScript. Before that, I was working with JavaScript and PHP, skills I picked up during my IT assistant training from 2019 to 2021. Once I started using TypeScript, I was hooked and have incorporated it into both personal and professional projects ever since.

react.name: React
react.description: I was introduced to ReactJS in 2021. Prior to this, I had experimented with EJS, a templating engine that allows embedding JavaScript code within HTML templates. While EJS served its purpose, discovering React's component-based architecture was a game-changer for me. I quickly fell in love with building dynamic and responsive user interfaces using React. Although I also explored Angular for a work project, I found that React's flexibility and simplicity resonated more with my development style. Despite Angular's robust features, my heart lies with React.

angular.name: Angular
angular.description: I was first introduced to AngularJS in 2023 during my apprenticeship. For a project, I built a small app with Angular to learn the basics. While I appreciated the experience, I prefer React for most of my projects, as it offers more freedom and flexibility. Angular is a great framework with many features, but React's simplicity and component-based approach appeal to me more. Nevertheless, I'm glad to have both technologies in my toolkit.

sass.name: SASS
sass.description: Although I had heard about SASS earlier, it wasn't until 2022 that I decided to give it a try. Once I did, I fell in love with its features. Since then, SASS has been my go-to for styling in every project. Prior to that, I was using standard CSS, a skill I developed during my IT assistant training.

nodejs.name: Node.js
nodejs.description: In 2022, I ventured into Node.js to write my first REST API. This experience opened up new possibilities, and I've since used Node.js for various small side projects. I also experimented with Bun, which I found quite enjoyable.

python.name: Python
python.description: My first attempt at learning Python was in 2018, but due to school commitments, I couldn't stick with it. A few years later, in 2022, I revisited Python and delved deeper. This renewed interest led me to develop a Blender plugin called "Node Runner", which facilitates importing and exporting shader nodes using text.

cpp.name: C++
cpp.description: During my initial IT assistant training between 2019 and 2021, C++ was the first programming language I learned. I used it to create console applications back then and later applied it at work in combination with Qt Widgets. Nowadays, I don't use C++ as often, as my focus has shifted towards web technologies. However, I still use it for some ESP32 microcontroller projects.

git.name: Git
git.description: I've been using Git for a long time; it's my go-to version control system for any kind of project. Its reliability makes it an indispensable tool in my development workflow.

blender.name: Blender
blender.description: I started actively using Blender in 2022 and quickly became enamored with the creative process of 3D modeling. This passion led me to develop the "Node Runner" plugin for shader nodes, enhancing my workflow within Blender.

giftboard.title: Giftboard
giftboard.description: Wishlist app for birthdays and occasions. Share a list, let others reserve gifts, and avoid duplicate presents while keeping surprises intact.

customParty.title: Custom Party App
customParty.description: Custom party app we built for my father-in-law's 49th birthday party. It features a 'find your partner' game, a quiz about him, a shared photo gallery and more. Built with React Native and TypeScript.
customParty.aria: Open Custom Party App project page

nolicom.title: ESP32 Retro Desk Notifier
nolicom.description: A  wireless desk device built with ESP32 and ESP-NOW which sends data (on button press) to notify the other device with sound and light to get the attention of the other person.
nolicom.aria: Open ESP32 Retro Desk Notifier project page
nolicom.imageAlt: ESP32 Retro Desk Notifier assembled prototype

aiChatSpeedBooster.title: AI Chat Speed Booster
aiChatSpeedBooster.description: Browser extension for ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, and custom sites. New fetch interception + LRU chat cache keeps long conversations fast.
aiChatSpeedBooster.aria: Open AI Chat Speed Booster project page
aiChatSpeedBooster.imageAlt: AI Chat Speed Booster popup screenshot

sshconfigLint.title: sshconfig-lint
sshconfigLint.description: Rust-based SSH config linter that catches common mistakes like duplicate hosts, weak algorithms, wildcard ordering issues, and Include cycles.

nodeRunner.title: Node Runner
nodeRunner.description: Blender addon + web platform to share, browse, save, and rate shader node trees.

hero.title: Giftboard
hero.tagline: A gift wishlist I built so <0>my girlfriend</0> doesn't have to coordinate everyone's presents anymore.

giftboard.tldr: Shared wishlist app. Add gifts, share the link, others reserve items secretly. React frontend, Node.js backend.

sections.whyP1: Every year around my birthday, my girlfriend had to coordinate all the gifts. Friends and family would ask her what I wanted, and she'd end up juggling a mental list of who's getting what - making sure nobody buys the same thing, while keeping everything a surprise from me.
sections.whyP2: It was stressful for her and not a great system. So I built Giftboard: I add what I'd like, share the link, and everyone can coordinate on their own. Reservations are hidden from me so the surprise stays intact and she can enjoy the birthday too.
sections.whatP1: You create a wishlist, add items with a link or your own image, and share it. If you paste an Amazon link the app automatically pulls the product image and price. Everyone except the wishlist owner can see which gifts are already reserved so no duplicates are purchased.
sections.whatP2: You can also set priority levels on wishes so people know what matters most to you.
sections.scrapeTitle: How price and image scraping works
sections.scrapeP1: When I paste a product link, the frontend sends it to a backend endpoint. That keeps scraping logic and anti-bot request headers on the server instead of exposing them in the browser.
sections.scrapeP2: I route this through POST so the payload is explicit in the request body and easier to validate. The server first sanitizes and verifies the URL, then blocks local or private-network targets to reduce SSRF risk.
sections.scrapeP3: After fetching the provided page, it chooses host-specific extraction rules, reads structured metadata/selectors, and returns only the normalized fields the app needs: price and image. Timeouts and null fallbacks keep the wishlist flow responsive even when scraping fails.
sections.scrapeP4: The selector rules come from a meta-sources.json file. For each supported shop, I map domains to the exact DOM selectors for price and image fields. This part is manual on purpose because every storefront has different markup. This is an example for Amazon domains:
sections.scrapeP5: I have to maintain these selectors manually, but that tradeoff gives me predictable extraction per domain and makes breakages easier to fix when a site changes its HTML.
sections.jsonIntro: This JSON is the actual persisted data model behind Giftboard. Whenever someone performs an action (for example adding a wish, editing details, or reserving an item), the REST API updates this structure and writes the latest state back to storage. I chose JSON here because this started as a small family project and I did not want to set up a full database for it. If usage grows later, I can migrate the same structure into a proper database. I split one full example into parts below so it is easier to understand what each field is used for.

gallery.heroAlt: Giftboard hero view in the alternate theme
gallery.wishesAlt: Giftboard wishes overview in the alternate theme
gallery.themeAlt: Giftboard theme preview in the alternate theme
gallery.createWishAlt: Giftboard create wish view in the alternate theme
gallery.boardAlt: Giftboard board overview in the alternate theme

how.amazonText: Paste a product link and the backend scrapes the image and price automatically.
how.customText: Not everything is on Amazon. You can upload your own picture for any wish.
how.hiddenText: Others mark what they'll buy and the owner never sees it.
how.prioritiesText: Flag how much you want each item so people can decide what to get.

tech.frontendText: React + TypeScript, styled with SCSS. Responsive and works on any screen size.
tech.backendText: A small REST API that scrapes Amazon product data and stores everything in a JSON file. I might switch to a database if it grows but this was simpler for now.

json.part1Text: Basic info about the wishlist itself: title, share code, owner and creation time.
json.part2Text: Each wish has title, priority, link/image data and quantity. Amazon links can include fetched image and price.
json.part3Text: Reservation fields keep track of who already committed to a gift, while the owner still cannot see that in the UI.

hero.title: Custom Party App
hero.tagline: A custom party app we built for my father-in-law's 49th birthday.

sections.whatText: This app was designed for one event: guests open a single link and get access to interactive activities. The goal was to make the party feel personal and playful, and to get everyone involved. Also because everybody got a "random" partner, it encouraged people to talk to others they did not know well.

features.partnerText: Guests solve clues and match with their assigned partner for the next activity.
features.quizText: A quiz focused on fun facts and stories about him.
features.galleryText: Everyone can upload and browse party photos in one place.

tech.text: Built with React Native + TypeScript to make the experience smooth on phones and fast to ship for a one-time event.

hero.title: ESP32 Retro Desk Notifier
hero.tagline: A wireless desk gadget I built so two people in the same office can still get each other's attention while both wearing headphones.

nolicom.tldr: Two ESP32. Press the button, the other device buzzes and lights up. Retro enclosure designed in Blender and resin-printed. Using ESP-NOW to get attention across the desks without Wi-Fi or delay.

problem.title: Problem
problem.p1: My girlfriend and I share one office room, and most days we both wear headphones. Calling each other didn't work, waving didn't work, flashing random lights didn't work - the only reliable option was standing up and walking around the desk.
problem.p2: That tiny interruption happened often enough to be genuinely annoying. I wanted a one-tap way to get attention without talking over music or getting up.
problem.p3: So I built a pair of desk devices: press the button <0 /> sound and light on the other desk.

concept.title: Concept
concept.p1: The idea is pretty simple: two identical units, one per desk, both battery powered and USB-C chargeable. Both devices can send and receive.
concept.p2: Press the button on one device <0 /> the other plays a sound and blinks its LEDs. No Wi-Fi router needed, just direct ESP-NOW peer-to-peer.
concept.p3: I wanted a retro look because I just love that style (also yes, I own a Commodore 64). I wanted it to feel more like a cool desk object than a generic tech gadget.

hardware.title: Hardware Overview
hardware.p1: I wanted the electronics to stay practical and repairable, so I used common, well-documented parts and built everything around predictable ESP32 I/O.
hardware.p2: The core setup is pretty straightforward:
hardware.p3: I started with a 2-segment display, but switched to 4 segments later because it gave me way more flexibility.
hardware.p4: The analog slider ended up being one of my favorite details. It makes the interaction feel less "digital gadget" and more like a tiny piece of desk hardware.

comms.title: Communication - ESP-NOW
comms.protocolSuffix: (direct peer-to-peer, no router required, fast and reliable).
comms.p1: Both ESP32 devices stay powered and connected all the time, so they can send tiny wireless signals instantly with no noticeable delay.
comms.flow1: Both devices boot and initialize ESP-NOW in station mode.
comms.flow2: Both devices stay awake and ready to communicate.
comms.flow3: When the button is pressed, a small ESP-NOW packet is sent.
comms.flow4: The receiver immediately triggers LEDs, sound, and display feedback.
comms.flow5: Feedback continues while button signals keep coming in.

prototype.title: Initial Prototype & Weird ESP Bug
prototype.p1: Before building the real device, I made a minimal prototype to learn pairing, packet flow, and latency behavior.
prototype.p2: Then I hit a super annoying bug: random button detection, random send behavior, and occasional looping on one ESP32. With my mobile oscilloscope I could see weird signal behavior.
prototype.p3: After ruling out wiring and software, I replaced the ESP32 module and everything worked reliably. So yeah - the first module was likely just faulty.
prototype.p4: That phase changed how I debug hardware: if behavior is inconsistent in ways software can't explain, test the physical module early, not last.

build.title: Full Prototype
build.p1: Once ESP-NOW was stable, I integrated everything into one complete breadboard prototype. That's when it finally felt real - button, buzzer, LEDs, potentiometer, and power system all running together.

workspace.title: The Workspace
workspace.p1: Most of the soldering, testing, and final assembly happened right at my hobby desk.

enclosure.title: Case Design
enclosure.p1: I modeled the case from scratch in Blender and iterated several times to get the proportions right. Priority was fitting battery + charging board first, then tuning openings for speaker, potentiometer slot, and USB-C access.
enclosure.p2: I aimed for compact retro proportions, internal mounting posts, and enough wall thickness so the case still feels solid.
enclosure.p3: <0>Not so fun fact:</0> after I switched to Arch Linux, I realized I hadn't backed up the original .blend file. All I have left now are the .goo slicer files.

printing.title: 3D Printing
printing.p1: PLA gave me cheap iterations, fast fit checks, and a cleaner workflow. Resin gave me the final surface quality and those nice sharp edges for the retro look.
printing.p2: Resin is honestly annoying to work with: constant gloves, changing gloves, smell, mask, cleanup. The result looks <0>incredible</0>, but if I'm honest, a really clean PLA print with more post-processing might be my favorite route next time.

finishing.title: Finale
finishing.p1: I painted the top in retro beige and the bottom in black. Looked great at first - then cracks showed up. Probably too much paint or primer mismatch.
finishing.p2: Then the final accident happened: I accidentally kicked it down the stairs. Electronics survived, resin case did not.

lessons.title: Lessons Learned
lessons.techText: ESP-NOW is awesome for ultra-local low-power communication. One thing I underestimated: sleep-state handling needs careful design, and ADC <0 /> PWM brightness mapping is super reliable once dialed in.
lessons.mechText: Resin looks premium but is way more annoying than PLA. Paint/primer/cure compatibility matters way more than expected. Also: analog controls like the potentiometer just feel better in daily use.

future.title: Future Improvements
future.p1: If I rebuild this (very likely), I'd add thicker internal supports and softer edges for durability, improve the primer/paint workflow, maybe use a vibration motor instead of a buzzer, and test a magnetic desk mount with a more impact-resistant case material.

conclusion.title: Conclusion
conclusion.p1: This project solved a real desk problem with one simple interaction: <0>press the button <1 /> light + sound</0>.
conclusion.p2: It combines embedded systems, low-power wireless communication, 3D modeling, printing, analog control, and a lot of practical debugging. Even with one staircase tragedy, it worked - totally worth building.

hero.title: AI Chat Speed Booster
hero.tagline: A browser extension that keeps long chats fast on ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, and custom AI sites using fetch interception, lazy rendering, and an LRU chat cache.

sections.whatText: AI Chat Speed Booster keeps large conversations responsive by rendering only recent messages first, then loading older ones on demand. The latest rewrite added fetch interception to trim heavy chat payloads before render, plus an LRU cache for faster chat switching. The project is fully open source on GitHub.

how.lazyText: Fast Mode intercepts chat/API responses and trims older turns before the app renders them, reducing load time in very long conversations.
how.hiddenText: The extension keeps up to 5 recent chats in an LRU cache so switching between active chats feels instant.
how.loadMoreText: Recent messages appear first. Older messages are hidden and can be revealed at the top in configurable batches.
how.fifoText: Site definitions live in one config file and currently include tested support for ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini.

settings.visibleText: Available via the official browser stores with automatic updates. Manual zip install is also supported.
settings.batchText: Visible messages (1-200), Load More batch (1-50), status indicator toggle, and badge position for 4 corners.
settings.toggleText: Add any AI chat app by editing one sites.config.json entry (hostnames, URL patterns, selectors) and rebuilding.
settings.reviewDelay: When AI chat websites change their UI, extension fixes can take a few days to appear in the Chrome Web Store because updates go through review checks.
settings.githubRelease: The latest GitHub release is usually available first and should work immediately while store review is pending.

privacy.noRead: No message content is ever read, stored, or transmitted.
privacy.noAnalytics: No analytics, telemetry, or external network requests.
privacy.localStorage: All settings stay in your browser's local storage.

tech.text: TypeScript + esbuild browser extension. Content scripts manage DOM visibility and fetch interception, background scripts broadcast config updates, and build scripts inject URL patterns into all browser manifests from one site config source.

image.alt: AI Chat Speed Booster extension popup
image.caption: Extension popup

build.p1: Build targets: Chrome, Firefox, Safari, and Edge. Node.js 18+ is required. You can run npm run build:all or build one target (for example npm run build:chrome).
build.p2: Automated tests use Playwright and cover dist validation, extension behavior on mock pages, and optional live integration tests with account auth profile reuse.
build.p3: The latest major upgrade focused on fetch interception, LRU chat caching, and stable support for Gemini + Claude while keeping the extension fully local and privacy-first.

code.intro: Core pieces from the rewritten architecture. Everything runs locally in the browser extension runtime.
code.messageManagerText: Fast Mode intercepts JSON responses, trims old turns, and returns a lighter response body so chats stay responsive.
code.fifoText: A small LRU cache keeps the 5 most recent chat DOM/message snapshots for fast chat switching.
code.domObserverText: Hostnames and selectors are resolved from a shared sites config so ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, and new sites can reuse the same pipeline.
code.backgroundText: Background messaging broadcasts config updates to all tabs that match injected URL patterns across supported AI chat sites.

hero.title: sshconfig-lint
hero.tagline: A Rust SSH config linter that checks ~/.ssh/config for common errors before they break your SSH workflow.

sections.whatText: sshconfig-lint scans your SSH config and included files for practical issues: duplicate Host blocks, missing IdentityFile paths, wildcard ordering, weak algorithms, duplicate directives, insecure options, unsafe ControlPath patterns, and Include cycles. Output is deterministic and sorted by file and line for clean CI diffs.

install.scriptText: Quick installer from the repository. You can override VERSION or INSTALL_DIR.
install.brewText: Install via tap, then optionally untap to keep your tap list clean.
install.cargoText: Install directly from crates.io using cargo.

usage.intro: CLI supports normal linting, explicit config file paths, JSON output, strict CI mode, and optional Include skipping.

rules.intro: Each finding has a stable code so you can grep or match in scripts. Example rules include:

dev.intro: The codebase is organized for parser/linter maintainability and easy rule expansion.

hero.title: Node Runner
hero.tagline: A Blender addon and companion website to export/import shader node trees and share them with the community.

sections.whatText: Node Runner is a Blender addon that lets you export selected nodes as text instead of sending a .blend file. It works across different node tree types and pairs with node-runner.thiering.org where you can upload and discover node trees for free.

media.uploadAlt: Node Runner uploaded node tree view
media.uploadCaption: View and manage an uploaded node tree

sharing.p1: You can upload node trees for free without creating an account.
sharing.p2: If you want to save and edit your uploads later, you can sign in for free with GitHub or Google.

features.exportFormats: Export selected nodes as Hash, JSON, AI JSON (stripped JSON for AI), or XML.
features.import: Import shared node data back into Blender.
features.treeTypes: Supports all kinds of Blender node trees.
features.clipboard: Paste node data directly from the clipboard.
features.imagePaths: Exports linked image paths as absolute paths.

export.step1: Node Editor > Select Nodes > Right Click > Node Runner > Export
export.step2: The exported result is copied directly to your clipboard.

import.step1: Node Editor > Right Click > Node Runner > Import
import.step2: If exported text is already in your clipboard: Node Editor > Right Click > Node Runner > Paste from Clipboard
import.step3: Paste from clipboard is the fastest route for most shared snippets.

how.export: Export selected shader nodes from Blender into a compact shareable string.
how.import: Import node trees back into Blender using Node Runner Import.
how.share: Share strings directly or publish trees on node-runner.thiering.org.
how.account: Guest uploads are free; login with GitHub/Google to edit and keep your uploads.
how.social: Community users can view, save, and rate uploaded node trees.

aiPrompt.intro: To generate valid Node Runner AI JSON with an LLM, prepend your request with the addon prompt and require strict JSON output.
aiPrompt.rules: Output only JSON (no explanation, no code fences). Use valid Blender bl_idname values, keep links/socket names exact, and include only non-default inputs/settings when possible.
